title: Fragment IV.

source.title: ANF06. Fathers of the Third Century: Gregory Thaumaturgus, Dionysius the Great, Julius Africanus, Anatolius, and Minor Writers, Methodius, Arnobius

verses.book: Some Other Fragments of the Same Methodius.
verses.text: Seest thou how, at the end of the contest, with a loud proclamation he declares the praises of the combatant, and discovers that which was in his afflictions hidden, in the words: “Thinkest thou that I had else answered thee, but that thou shouldest appear just?”

verses.book: Some Other Fragments of the Same Methodius.
verses.text: This is the salve of his wounds, this the reward of his patience.

verses.book: Some Other Fragments of the Same Methodius.
verses.text: For as to what followed, although he received double his former possessions, these may seem to have been given him by divine providence as small indeed, and for trifling causes, even though to some they may appear great.

verses.book: Some Other Fragments of the Same Methodius.
Some Other Fragments of the Same Methodius.
verses.text: Thou contendest with Me, and settest thyself against Me, and opposest those who combat for Me.

verses.book: Some Other Fragments of the Same Methodius.
verses.text: But where wert thou when I made the world?

verses.book: Some Other Fragments of the Same Methodius.
Some Other Fragments of the Same Methodius.
verses.text: Hadst thou yet, says He, fallen from thy mother? for there was darkness, in the beginning of the world’s creation, He says, upon the face of the deep.

verses.book: Some Other Fragments of the Same Methodius.
verses.text: Now this darkness was no created darkness, but one which of set purpose had place, by reason of the absence of light.
